No Poverty.reason: The course's methods for "identifying hazard zones" and "evaluating land-use policy scenarios" can be applied to build resilience for the poor (Target 1.5) and manage land access (1.4).

Zero Hunger.reason: The technique of "evaluating land-use policy scenarios" is applicable to designing resilient agricultural practices (Target 2.4), though agriculture is not an explicit focus of the course.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The "Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)" use case and methods for "identifying hazard zones" directly support reducing illnesses from environmental pollution and contamination, linking to Target 3.9.

Quality Education.reason: The course provides knowledge and skills for sustainable development by tasking students with developing an "SDG spatial MCDM solution," directly addressing Target 4.7.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: GIS methods taught are essential for integrated water resource management (Target 6.5). "Identifying hazard zones" and "EIA" are applicable to water quality and ecosystem protection.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: The course explicitly covers "facility sites and infrastructure planning," a core task for developing renewable energy infrastructure and expanding energy access (Targets 7.2, 7.b).

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course teaches "spatial machine learning models" and other advanced methods, contributing to the technological upgrading and innovation needed for higher economic productivity (Target 8.2).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: Directly addresses developing resilient infrastructure through "infrastructure planning" (Target 9.1) and promotes innovation using "GIS software" and "machine learning methods" (Target 9.5).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: Spatial analysis of "facility sites and infrastructure planning" can be used to promote equitable access to services, thus supporting social and economic inclusion (Target 10.2).

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: Core topics like "infrastructure planning," "identifying hazard zones," and "land-use policy" are fundamental to sustainable urban planning and disaster resilience (Targets 11.3, 11.5, 11.b).

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The "Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)" use case and "evaluating land-use policy scenarios" directly support the sustainable management and efficient use of natural resources (Target 12.2).

Climate Action.reason: Directly supports climate action by teaching methods for "identifying hazard zones" (Target 13.1) and integrating climate measures into "infrastructure planning" and "land-use policy" (Target 13.2).

Life Below Water.reason: The "EIA" and "land-use policy" methods are applicable to managing coastal ecosystems and land-based pollution affecting marine environments (Targets 14.1, 14.2), though not explicitly mentioned.

Life on Land.reason: Directly addresses land ecosystems through "evaluating land-use policy scenarios," "EIA," and extracting "land-cover information," which are key to conservation and sustainable use (Targets 15.1, 15.9).

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: Focuses on "structured decision-making" and "Spatial Decision Support Systems (SDSS)," which promote transparent, accountable, and participatory decision-making processes (Targets 16.6, 16.7).

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The task to develop an "SDG spatial MCDM solution" directly enhances policy coherence for sustainable development (Target 17.14) and builds data analysis capacity (Target 17.19).

gemini_critique.critique: No revisions needed. Model A's analysis was not provided, so there are no differences to assess.
